Object.Name: WiFi Client Management
Object.Description1: This LwM2M Object provides comprehensive management and monitoring of connected WiFi clients on OpenWRT-based access points. It enables real-time monitoring of client connection statistics including signal strength (RSSI), data rates, bandwidth usage, and connection duration. The object supports advanced WiFi management features such as client disconnection, MAC-based access control lists (whitelist/blacklist), band steering via 802.11v BSS Transition Management, QoS priority assignment, and client isolation. Integration with OpenWRT's hostapd, iwinfo, and ubus interfaces provides detailed per-client statistics including 802.11 capability detection, frequency band identification, and traffic monitoring. Essential for enterprise WiFi deployments requiring granular client control, network optimization, and security policy enforcement.

Item.Name: MAC Address
Item.Description: MAC address of the connected WiFi client in colon-separated hexadecimal format (e.g., "AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF"). This uniquely identifies each client device on the wireless network. Read-only value automatically populated when the client associates with the access point.

Item.Name: IP Address
Item.Description: IPv4 address assigned to the client (e.g., "192.168.1.100"). Obtained from ARP cache or DHCP lease table. May be empty if the client hasn't completed network layer configuration or is using IPv6 only.

Item.Name: Hostname
Item.Description: Hostname of the client device as provided during DHCP negotiation or resolved via DNS/mDNS. Useful for identifying devices by human-readable names. Shows "unknown" if hostname information is unavailable.

Item.Name: Interface
Item.Description: Wireless interface to which this client is connected (e.g., "wlan0", "wlan1"). Identifies which physical or virtual access point interface is serving this client, important for multi-SSID or multi-radio configurations.

Item.Name: Signal Strength
Item.Description: Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI) in dBm (typical range: -100 to -30 dBm). Negative values closer to 0 indicate stronger signals. Values above -50 dBm are excellent, -50 to -60 dBm good, -60 to -70 dBm fair, below -70 dBm poor. Essential for troubleshooting connectivity issues and optimizing AP placement.

Item.Name: Noise Level
Item.Description: Background noise level on the wireless channel in dBm. Represents environmental RF interference. Typical WiFi noise floors range from -95 to -85 dBm. Used to calculate Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR).

Item.Name: SNR
Item.Description: Signal-to-Noise Ratio in dB, calculated as (RSSI - Noise Level). Indicates signal quality relative to background noise. Higher values are better: >40 dB excellent, 25-40 dB good, 15-25 dB fair, <15 dB poor. More reliable than RSSI alone for assessing connection quality.

Item.Name: TX Rate
Item.Description: Current transmit data rate from access point to client in Mbps. Dynamically adjusted based on signal quality and channel conditions. Maximum rates depend on WiFi standard: 802.11b (11 Mbps), 802.11g (54 Mbps), 802.11n (600 Mbps), 802.11ac (6933 Mbps), 802.11ax (9608 Mbps).

Item.Name: RX Rate
Item.Description: Current receive data rate from client to access point in Mbps. Dynamically adjusted based on signal quality and channel conditions. May differ from TX rate due to asymmetric channel conditions or client capabilities.

Item.Name: TX Bytes
Item.Description: Total number of bytes transmitted from access point to client since connection establishment. Monotonically increasing counter useful for bandwidth usage monitoring and accounting. Resets to zero when client reconnects.

Item.Name: RX Bytes
Item.Description: Total number of bytes received by access point from client since connection establishment. Monotonically increasing counter useful for bandwidth usage monitoring and accounting. Resets to zero when client reconnects.

Item.Name: Connected Time
Item.Description: Duration in seconds since the client associated with the access point. Useful for session tracking and identifying long-running vs. transient connections. Resets when client reconnects.

Item.Name: Capability
Item.Description: Highest 802.11 standard supported by the client as negotiated during association. Possible values: "802.11b", "802.11g", "802.11a", "802.11n", "802.11ac", "802.11ax". Parsed from client capability flags. Helps identify client device capabilities and potential performance bottlenecks.

Item.Name: Band
Item.Description: Frequency band currently used by the client. Possible values: "2.4GHz" (2412-2484 MHz), "5GHz" (5170-5835 MHz), "6GHz" (5955-7115 MHz). Determined from the operating channel frequency. Essential for band steering operations and spectrum management.

Item.Name: Bandwidth
Item.Description: Channel bandwidth currently negotiated with the client in MHz. Possible values: 20 (legacy), 40 (802.11n+), 80 (802.11ac+), 160 (802.11ac/ax). Wider bandwidths provide higher throughput but are more susceptible to interference. Parsed from client capability flags.

Item.Name: ACL State
Item.Description: Access Control List state for this client. Values: "none" (no ACL policy applied), "allowed" (explicitly whitelisted), "denied" (explicitly blacklisted). Read-write resource that allows querying and modifying ACL state. Changes are applied to hostapd ACL configuration.

Item.Name: QoS Priority
Item.Description: Quality of Service priority level for this client (0-7, where 7 is highest priority). Maps to WMM/802.11e traffic categories. Higher priority clients receive preferential treatment for channel access and bandwidth allocation. Useful for prioritizing VoIP, video streaming, or critical IoT devices over best-effort traffic.

Item.Name: Isolated
Item.Description: Client isolation flag. When true, this client is prevented from communicating directly with other wireless clients on the same SSID (AP isolation). Client can only communicate with the wired network/gateway. Enhances security for guest networks and public WiFi by preventing peer-to-peer attacks. False allows normal client-to-client communication.

Item.Name: Last Activity
Item.Description: Time in seconds since the last frame transmission or reception from this client. Indicates client activity level. Low values (<10 seconds) indicate active communication, high values suggest idle or sleeping client. Useful for identifying stale connections and implementing inactivity-based disconnection policies.

Item.Name: Disconnect Client
Item.Description: Execute this resource to forcibly disconnect the client from the access point. Sends 802.11 deauthentication frame via hostapd, immediately terminating the client's association. Client may automatically reconnect unless blocked by ACL. Useful for enforcing policies, troubleshooting, or manually managing network access.

Item.Name: Add to Blacklist
Item.Description: Execute this resource to add the client's MAC address to the hostapd deny ACL (blacklist) and immediately disconnect it. Prevents the client from reconnecting until removed from the blacklist. Also sets ACL State to "denied". Useful for blocking unauthorized or problematic devices. Blacklist persists in hostapd runtime configuration but may need to be saved to UCI for persistence across reboots.

Item.Name: Add to Whitelist
Item.Description: Execute this resource to add the client's MAC address to the hostapd accept ACL (whitelist). When whitelist mode is enabled on the AP, only whitelisted clients can connect. Also removes the MAC from deny list if present and sets ACL State to "allowed". Useful for implementing strict MAC-based authentication. Whitelist persists in hostapd runtime configuration but may need to be saved to UCI for persistence across reboots.

Item.Name: Steer to Band
Item.Description: Execute this resource to steer the client to a different frequency band using 802.11v BSS Transition Management Request. Parameter should be target band: "2.4", "5", or "6" (as string). Sends BSS-TM request frame suggesting the client transition to an AP on the specified band. Requires 802.11v support in both AP (hostapd) and client device. Client may honor or ignore the request. Useful for load balancing and optimizing dual-band/tri-band deployments by moving capable clients to less congested bands.
